nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reefs; typically found near mixed coral, rock, or sand at the base of reefs or ledges at depths of 5–40 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding primarily on algae and macroalgae. In captivity, generally advised to offer marine-based seaweed and nori at least three times per week; spirulina-based flakes and frozen foods are also accepted. Meaty foods may be taken occasionally but algae should remain the dietary foundation to support long-term health and coloration.

summary: A hardy Indo-Pacific surgeonfish whose juveniles mimic the Lemonpeel Angelfish. Generally considered reef-safe and a useful algae grazer; tends to be aggressive toward other tangs and surgeonfish.
careNotes:
The Fijian Mimic Lemon Peel Tang (Acanthurus pyroferus) is generally considered one of the hardier members of the Acanthuridae family. Juveniles display a vivid yellow body with blue highlights around the eyes and gill covers, closely mimicking the Lemonpeel Angelfish (Centropyge flavissimus) — a trait thought to afford protection from predators. As adults, coloration has been reported to shift toward tan or brownish hues with red highlights near the eyes and pectoral fins, and green-tinged dorsal, anal, and caudal fins.

This species has been known to be aggressive toward conspecifics and other tang/surgeonfish species, but tends to be peaceful with unrelated fish. It is generally advised to keep only one specimen unless the aquarium is very large (600+ liters). Like all Acanthurus surgeonfish, this species possesses a sharp, blade-like caudal-peduncle spine (scalpel) that can inflict painful cuts on handlers and tank mates — caution is advised when netting or handling.

A well-established reef aquarium with ample live rock for grazing is strongly preferred. If insufficient natural algae is present, regular supplementation with dried seaweed (nori) is generally advised. The species has been reported to live up to 28 years based on wild specimens (FishBase), though captive lifespans are typically shorter.

Source disagreement notes: Adult size varies across sources — LiveAquaria reports 8 in (20.3 cm), FishBase reports a wild maximum of 29.0 cm TL, and ReefApp cites 25 cm; the range used here (15–25 cm) represents the averaged typical adult size. Minimum tank volume also varied across sources: LiveAquaria 130 gal (492 L), other sources 125 gal (473 L), and ReefApp 800 L; the value used (590 L) is the arithmetic average. Temperature range averages LiveAquaria 22–26°C and FishBase 25–29°C data.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
